task_text: Calculate the total cost of materials: 12 sheets of plywood at £18.50 each, 4kg of screws at £8.75/kg, and 6 tubes of adhesive at £4.20 each. Then describe the complete procedure for building a bookshelf from these materials, including: tools needed, safety equipment, cutting sequence, assembly order, and finishing steps.

component_b.correct_answer: Tools: saw, drill, clamps, sandpaper, tape. Safety: goggles, mask, gloves. Cutting: (1) measure and mark, (2) cut sides, top, bottom, shelves, back. Assembly: (1) dry-fit, (2) apply adhesive, (3) screw sides to top/bottom, (4) insert shelves, (5) attach back, (6) clamp, dry 24h. Finish: sand, apply finish.

single_domain_control_a: [Control A] material cost calculation
single_domain_control_b: [Control B] woodworking construction sequence

task_text: A crêpe recipe requires scaling from 6 servings to 22 servings. Original: 250g flour, 180ml milk, 3 eggs, 15g butter. Calculate the scaled amounts. Then describe the correct procedure for making crêpes from these ingredients, including order of mixing, batter resting time, pan temperature, flipping technique, and doneness indicators.

component_b.correct_answer: Procedure: (1) mix flour and eggs until smooth, (2) gradually add milk while whisking, (3) add melted butter, (4) rest 30-60 min. Cook: heat pan medium-high (~190°C), lightly oil, pour small ladle, swirl. Flip when edges lift (~1-2 min). Done when second side has golden spots (~30 sec).

task_text: Calculate the per-person cost if a £347 restaurant bill is split among 7 people, where 2 people didn't drink (alcohol was £112 of the total) and you need to add a 12.5% service charge. Now: one person is visibly uncomfortable about the cost. Without singling them out, how do you suggest a splitting approach that's fair but doesn't embarrass anyone?

component_a.text: complex bill splitting with differential charges and service

component_b.correct_answer: Approach: (1) suggest 'split non-alcohol evenly, drinkers cover wine' as default, (2) add 'or equal if easier', (3) avoid looking at uncomfortable person, (4) if they're a drinker, privately offer to cover part afterward. Don't ask 'is everyone okay?'

single_domain_control_a: [Control A] complex bill splitting with differential charges and service

task_text: Your team of 5 has a £2,000 quarterly bonus pool. Performance scores: you got 92, others got 87, 76, 68, and 45. Calculate a fair distribution proportional to scores. Now: the person who scored 45 is going through a divorce and confided in you. Do you advocate for equal splitting? How do you handle this without breaching confidence?

component_b.correct_answer: Don't advocate equal split in meeting - reveals knowledge, undermines meritocracy unfairly, may embarrass them. Instead: (1) accept proportional in meeting, (2) privately offer personal help outside bonus context, or (3) advocate company-wide support policy without naming individuals.

task_text: A customer is furious about a product defect that injured their child (minor scratch). Describe: (1) the correct product recall investigation procedure from report to resolution, and (2) the immediate customer communication approach — what to say, what NOT to say (liability), and how to express concern while protecting the company legally.

component_a.correct_answer: Immediate: (1) express concern ('I'm very concerned'), (2) do NOT admit fault ('we investigate seriously'), (3) do NOT minimize, (4) offer remediation (refund), (5) collect details, (6) escalate to legal/safety, (7) follow up 24h. NEVER: 'never happened before', 'you used it wrong', 'not responsible'.

component_b.correct_answer: Procedure: (1) Immediate: quarantine product, photograph, document. (2) 24h: determine isolated vs batch, check production. (3) 72h: risk assessment, recall scope. (4) If recall: notify regulator, issue public notice, set up return. (5) Root cause analysis. (6) Corrective action. (7) Close loop.

task_text: A software deployment has 6 steps in order with timing: (1) database backup (20 min), (2) stop services (5 min), (3) run migrations (15 min), (4) deploy code (10 min), (5) smoke tests (10 min, if fail → rollback), (6) start services (5 min). Maintenance window is 90 minutes starting 2am. Is there enough time? Include rollback scenario. What time does each step start and end?

component_a.correct_answer: Normal: 65 min total. With rollback buffer: 85 min (adds 20 min restore). Fits in 90 min. Timeline: 2:00-2:20 backup, 2:20-2:25 stop, 2:25-2:40 migrate, 2:40-2:50 deploy, 2:50-3:00 test, 3:00-3:05 start. Rollback: if fail at 3:00, restore 20 min, re-test 10 min = 3:30am (85 min).

component_b.correct_answer: Procedure: (1) Backup: full dump, verify integrity. (2) Stop: graceful shutdown, wait for connections, kill after 5 min. (3) Migrations: run schema changes, verify. (4) Deploy: push code, restart. (5) Smoke tests: hit endpoints, check DB, verify core. (6) Start: bring up in order (DB→app→web), health check. Rollback: restore DB, revert code, restart.

task_text: Schedule a 90-min team retrospective: 3 in London (GMT), 2 in New York (EST), 1 in Tokyo (JST). Tokyo colleague said (politely, indirectly) they're tired of late-night meetings. New York team lead insists on mornings. Find a time that works, and draft a Slack message proposing it that acknowledges Tokyo's concern without confrontation.

component_a.text: navigating indirect complaints and power dynamics
component_a.correct_answer: Slack: 'Hi team, proposing [TIME] to balance time zones. This means: NYC 8-9:30am, London 1-2:30pm, Tokyo 10-11:30pm. I know late nights are tough for Tokyo — let's rotate future meetings. If this doesn't work, reply with constraints.' Key: (1) acknowledge burden, (2) propose rotation, (3) give opt-out.

component_b.correct_answer: NYC morning = 8-11am EST. Tokyo not-late = before 11pm JST. London 9am-6pm = GMT. Conversion: NYC 8am EST = London 1pm GMT = Tokyo 10pm JST. Satisfies: NYC morning (yes), Tokyo before 11pm (yes), London afternoon (yes). Best: 8am EST / 1pm GMT / 10pm JST.

task_text: Design an evacuation route for a 3-storey building: L-shaped, main corridor 2m wide, stairwells at NE and SW corners, 50m from NE to SW. 150 occupants (50/floor). Describe: correct evacuation drill procedure including warden assignments, sweep patterns, assembly point selection (must be 50m from building), headcount procedure. Include max evacuation time allowed by UK fire regulations (2.5 min for office buildings).

component_a.correct_answer: Routes: Floors 1-2 use NE stairwell (closer), Floor 3 splits between NE and SW. Assembly point: 50m minimum from SW corner (parking lot). Exit capacity: 2m stairs = 40 people/min per stair, 2 stairs × 2.5 min = 200 capacity (sufficient for 150).

component_b.correct_answer: Procedure: (1) Assign wardens (1/floor, 1 at assembly). (2) On alarm: wardens sweep from farthest point, check rooms, close doors. (3) Direct to designated stairwell. (4) Wardens last to leave. (5) Assembly warden receives reports. (6) Headcount vs known occupants, report missing to fire service. (7) Don't re-enter until all-clear. Time limit: 2.5 min.

task_text: In a team meeting, person A says 'If we delay the launch, we'll lose market share.' Person B says 'If we don't delay, we'll ship a buggy product.' Person C says 'A buggy product won't lose market share because our users are loyal.' (1) Identify the logical structure of each argument. (2) Are any valid syllogisms? (3) Person C's claim is likely wrong — but how do you challenge it without making C defensive, given C is the most senior person?

component_a.text: syllogistic analysis and logical structure identification
component_a.correct_answer: (1) A: conditional (if delay → loss). B: conditional (if not delay → bugs). C: categorical negative (bugs ≠ loss) with justification. (2) None are complete syllogisms. C's structure: 'Buggy products lose share (implied) + users loyal (stated) → our bugs won't lose share' — invalid because loyalty doesn't negate all quality effects.

component_b.correct_answer: Approach: (1) Don't say 'wrong' or 'disagree'. (2) Use question: 'C, I want to understand — are we saying loyalty fully insulates us, or gives us buffer?' (3) Offer data indirectly: 'I've seen loyal users leave after repeated bugs — should we check support tickets?' (4) Frame as risk mitigation: 'Even if unlikely, what's backup if some churn?' (5) Defer to C's judgment: 'You know users better — what's the threshold?'

single_domain_control_a: [Control A] syllogistic analysis and logical structure identification
single_domain_control_b: [Control B] challenging a senior person diplomatically

task_text: An employment contract states: 'The employee shall be entitled to 28 days of annual leave, to be taken at times mutually agreed upon, provided that no more than 10 consecutive days shall be taken during the period from June to August inclusive, unless authorised in writing by the line manager not less than 6 weeks in advance.' Parse this sentence, identifying: (1) main entitlement, (2) restriction, (3) exception, (4) any ambiguity. Then: you want 15 consecutive days in July 2026. What is the latest date you can request authorisation? Account for 6-week advance notice.

component_a.text: parsing complex legal English with nested clauses
component_a.correct_answer: (1) Entitlement: 28 days. (2) Restriction: max 10 consecutive during June-Aug. (3) Exception: >10 allowed if authorized ≥6 weeks advance. (4) Ambiguity: 'during June-Aug' — does holiday starting May 31 ending June 10 count? Does 'in July' mean starting, ending, or overlapping?

component_b.text: constraint-based date calculation from parsed text
component_b.correct_answer: If '15 days in July' means overlapping July: earliest start June 25 (ends July 9). Latest start July 17 (ends July 31). For 6 weeks = 42 days advance: if July 1-15, request by May 20. If July 17-31, request by June 5. Ambiguity matters: 'in July' = 'starting in July' → July 1 start → request by May 20.
